instruction: What is Atomic functions# in AMD ROCm/HIP development?
output:
Atomic functions are read-modify-write (RMW) operations, whose result is visible
to all other threads on the scope of the atomic operation, once the operation
completes.
If multiple instructions from different devices or threads target the same
memory location, the instructions are serialized in an undefined order.
Atomic operations in kernels can operate on block scope (i.e. shared memory),
device scope (global memory), or system scope (system memory), depending onhardware support.
The listed functions are also available with the_system(e.g.atomicAdd_system) suffix, operating on system scope, which includes host
memory and other GPUs’ memory. The functions without suffix operate on shared
or global memory on the executing device, depending on the memory space of the
variable.
HIP supports the following atomic operations, whereTYPEis one ofint,unsignedint,unsignedlong,unsignedlonglong,floatordouble, whileINTEGERisint,unsignedint,unsignedlong,unsignedlonglong:

instruction: [Documentation]: Stream to SW Queue/HW queue mapping?
input:
### Description of errors

The mapping of SW queues to HW queues leads to queue contention.

From the logs:
```
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3140: 1917470247183d us:  Created SWq=0x7fd9ee22c000 to map on HWq=0x7fd9c2800000 with size 16384 with priority 1, cooperative: 0
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3232: 1917470247195d us:  acquireQueue refCount: 0x7fd9c2800000 (1)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3140: 1917470569618d us:  Created SWq=0x7fd9ee216000 to map on HWq=0x7fd9c2000000 with size 16384 with priority 1, cooperative: 0
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3232: 1917470569624d us:  acquireQueue refCount: 0x7fd9c2000000 (1)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3140: 1917486812683d us:  Created SWq=0x7fd9ee17e000 to map on HWq=0x7fd9c0400000 with size 16384 with priority 1, cooperative: 0
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3232: 1917486812689d us:  acquireQueue refCount: 0x7fd9c0400000 (1)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3140: 1917486820749d us:  Created SWq=0x7fd9ee16c000 to map on HWq=0x7fd9b0600000 with size 16384 with priority 1, cooperative: 0
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3232: 1917486820754d us:  acquireQueue refCount: 0x7fd9b0600000 (1)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3046: 1917486821468d us:  Selected queue refCount: 0x7fd9b0600000 (2)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3046: 1917486822192d us:  Selected queue refCount: 0x7fd9c0400000 (2)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3046: 1917486822857d us:  Selected queue refCount: 0x7fd9c2000000 (2)
:3:rocdevice.cpp            :3046: 1917486823563d us:  Selected que
output:
If you allocate GPU_MAX_HW_QUEUES streams, you'll also create the GPU_MAX_HW_QUEUES underlying queues. Once done, free all the streams. The HW queues are not setup and no stream are created (except null stream).
Then allocate GPU_MAX_HW_QUEUES, observe that the streams are all in order. Stream zero is on queue 0, stream 1 on queue 1 etc.

See, kernel <3> and <4> use stream 3 and 4 respectively, they run on the same HW queue leading to contention. Not that the kernel here use only one CU so no real duration increase is observed.
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d6db9eb9-fbf5-40df-8f9d-64ea63977c86)
After freeing the streams and reallocating we get a weird mapping but, at least, a correct one.
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a6fd7777-0db1-4edf-9302-b2c4e0ddd2c8)

Source: https://github.com/ROCm/hip/issues/3769

instruction: [Issue]: `hipMemCreate` failing in `hip-tests/catch/unit/virtualMemoryManagement`?
input:
### Problem Description

# Description

`hip-tests/catch/unit/virtualMemoryManagement/hipMemCreate` test is failing.

The ROCm and HIP version is based on this docker image `rocm/dev-ubuntu-22.04:6.3.1-complete`.

```
root:/app/hip-tests/build/catch_tests/unit/virtualMemoryManagement# ./VirtualMemoryManagementTest 

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
VirtualMemoryManagementTest is a Catch v2.13.4 host application.
Run with -? for options

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Unit_hipMemCreate_MapNonContiguousChunks
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
/app/hip-tests/catch/unit/virtualMemoryManagement/hipMemCreate.cc:272
...............................................................................

/app/hip-tests/catch/unit/virtualMemoryManagement/hipMemCreate.cc:323: FAILED:
  REQUIRE( true == std::equal(B_h.begin(), B_h.end(), C_h.data()) )
with expansion:
  true == false

Memory access fault by GPU node-9 (Agent handle: 0x2373990) on address 0x7f88a6dd5000. Reason: Unknown.
GPU core dump created: gpucore.27623
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Unit_hipMemSetAccess_Vmm2UnifiedMemCpy
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
/app/hip-tests/catch/unit/virtualMemoryManagement/hipMemSetGetAccess.cc:552
................................................................
output:
Hi @tjtanaa, seems like the test `Unit_hipMemCreate_MapNonContiguousChunks` is actually being skipped right now (https://github.com/ROCm/hip-tests/blob/rocm-6.3.x/catch/hipTestMain/config/config_amd_linux#L133). 

Instead of directly running the test binary from the build folder, using `ctest` would properly skip the tests after running `cmake build_tests`. By default, ctest will run all tests available, but we can run a subset of tests using REGEX (e.g. `ctest -R hipMem` for Memory related tests.) 

Alternatively, to run all tests from the VirtualMemoryManagement folder exclusively, after running `cmake build_tests`, we can run `make VirtualMemoryManagement` followed by `make test`. This will be equivalent to directly running the test binary but with the proper configs applied. 

The second failure mentioned in your ticket is still being investigated. We will be tracking it further with #3763. 

Thanks!

Source: https://github.com/ROCm/hip/issues/3762

instruction: How do I optimize rocprof compute viewer# on AMD ROCm?
output:
ROCprof Compute Viewerprovides a GUI-based environment for analyzing GPU kernel performance data. It
delivers detailed kernel-level insights, including counter correlation and
hierarchical performance breakdowns, helping developers interpret execution
patterns and identify optimization opportunities.

instruction: How do I using hipify# when moving from CUDA to HIP?
output:
HIPIFYis a collection of tools that automatically
translate CUDA code to HIP code. For example,cuEventCreateis translated tohipEventCreate(). HIPIFY tools also convert error codes from the
driver namespace and coding conventions to the equivalent HIP error code.
HIP unifies the APIs for these common functions.
There are two types of HIPIFY available:
